sections.title: So Many Kinds of Plants
sections.content_summary: Through the story of three children walking to school, this section introduces the remarkable diversity of plants in the surroundings — plants of different sizes, shapes, stem types, and leaf textures. Trees are defined as plants with a big woody trunk, deep roots, and many branches with leaves; examples include mango, coconut, khejri, jackfruit, banyan, amaltas, peepal, and chinar. The section builds sensory awareness of plants through texture, smell, shade, and colour of leaves and fruits.

tasks.task_text: Write the names of trees that you can recognise. Try to remember where you have seen these trees. Which of these trees have you seen near your home or on your way to school?

sections.title: Shrubs
sections.content_summary: Shrubs are introduced as medium-sized plants with several woody stems and branches growing close to the ground — distinct from trees which have one big trunk. Examples given include hibiscus, rose, holy basil (tulsi), and curry leaf. An IKS connection is made: pulses eaten in everyday Indian meals — toor, masoor, moong, and urad — are seeds of shrubs, linking plant classification to food culture.

tasks.task_text: Write the names of some shrubs. Have you seen any of the shrubs shown in the pictures above?
Do you know what they are called in your mother tongue?

sections.title: Herbs and Grasses
sections.content_summary: Herbs are defined as smaller plants with soft stems that do not become woody; grasses are a type of herb with thin, flat, hollow-stemmed leaves. Examples of herbs include mint, tomato, coriander, and mustard. The section reveals culturally significant knowledge: staple food grains (paddy, wheat, bajra, jowar, ragi) are seeds of large grasses, and sugarcane and bamboo are tall grasses — connecting plant biology to Indian food and material traditions. Students are prompted to observe different grasses around them.

tasks.task_text: Write the names of some herbs that you have seen and where you have seen them.

sections.title: Climbers and Creepers
sections.content_summary: Climbers and creepers are plants with thin, flexible stems that cannot stand on their own. Climbers grow by climbing on other plants or structures for support (e.g. money plant, jasmine, bottle gourd), while creepers spread along the ground (e.g. watermelon, pumpkin). The section notes that some climbers even take food from the plant on which they climb. Students are asked to identify and name these plants in their local language, and to classify familiar plants (marigold, neem, jujube) by type.

tasks.task_text: Write the names of some climbers or creepers that you have seen and where you have seen them. Are any of them in the pictures given on page 53? What are they called in your language?
Name these plants in your language. Say if they are a tree, shrub, climber or creeper.

Stand, in groups of two to four, next to any tree or shrub that is in or near your school. Now look around, as far as you can see. Remember to look down near your feet as well! How many kinds of trees, shrubs, herbs, grasses, climbers or creepers can you spot?

sections.title: Parts of a Plant
sections.content_summary: This section consolidates learning about plant parts — roots, stems, leaves, flowers, fruits and seeds — by presenting a labelled diagram of a tomato plant. Students observe that different plants have leaves of varying colours, shapes, and sizes, with examples such as peepal, mango, lotus, and khejri leaves shown. Three extended activities deepen sensory engagement: Activity 2 asks students to befriend and care for a plant over time with sustained daily observation; Activity 3 prompts observation of leaf change, flowering, and fruiting over time; Activities 4 and 5 explore leaf and fruit smell, including a blindfold smell-identification game.

tasks.task_text: Make friends with a plant! Choose one plant, preferably a shrub with a thick stem or a tree that you want to make friends with. Name your plant, just like you might name a pet. Take care of the plant by watering it each day. Also protect it as your friend. Now observe your plant friend closely. Here are a few things to observe — its leaves, flowers and fruits. Are there many, a few or no leaves, flowers and fruits? Record your observations in the table.

Do you find new leaves growing on the plant? Do the colours of the leaves change as they grow larger? Do the old brown leaves fall to the ground? Do you find flowers appearing or notice any fruit? What are other observations you have? Note down your observations.
Write about your plant in your notebook.

Observe the leaves in your surroundings. Draw, colour and label them in your notebook. Describe their colour, shape, size, texture and smell to your friend in the class. What statements can we make about leaves after doing this activity?

Blindfold yourself while your friend gradually brings a fruit close to you. From how far can you recognise the fruit with your eyes closed? Now repeat the same experiment with pieces of cut fruit. Was it easier to recognise cut fruit by the smell rather than the whole (uncut) fruit? Which fruit could you smell from farthest away? Try this experiment at home too.

Get to know Barks. Bark is the hard outer covering of a tree trunk. Touch and look carefully at the bark of a tree. Do you see any animals, insects or plants on it? Press a sheet of paper on the bark. Gently move a crayon or a pencil repeatedly on it. See what you have! Write the name (or description) of your tree on the reverse of the paper. Now collect all the papers of your friends and see if you can guess the tree just by looking at its bark pattern.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The closing reflection section consolidates learning through discussion, writing, drawing, and a creative activity. Students discuss the importance of plants, the role of roots and stems, and list plants from their immediate environment by type. They describe their favourite plant and draw different leaf types. The section ends with a creative IKS-linked activity: making rangoli patterns using fallen leaves and flowers, and creating animal shapes from collected leaves — connecting plant observation to Indian artistic traditions.

tasks.task_text: What would happen if there were no plants?
How does the root help a plant to grow?
What is the role of the stem?
List the names of plants that you have seen in your school, park or near your home. Identify what types of plants they are — tree, shrub, herb, grass, climber or creeper.
Which particular part of the plant helped you to identify the type of the plant?
Describe your favourite plant. Why is it your favourite?
Draw different types of leaves you have seen around you.
Collect leaves and flowers fallen on the ground. Arrange them in patterns to make a rangoli. You may also create different animal shapes using the collected leaves.

dual_strand.natural: Diversity of plant types and their physical features — trees, shrubs, herbs, grasses, climbers, creepers — and parts of a plant
dual_strand.human_cultural: Human use, naming, and care of plants across Indian cultural contexts — local names in mother tongue, pulses and grains as food, rangoli and leaf art traditions
